did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a lightweight hybrid deep learning model called Compact Convolutional Transformer (CCT), which combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs), to perform skin lesion classification (benign vs. malignant) on dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new lightweight hybrid model (Compact CNN-Transformer) for skin lesion classification. It includes a methodology section describing the model architecture, an experimental section with implementation settings and hyperparameters, and results sections detailing performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, AUC) and comparative analysis against benchmark models on the HAM10000 dataset. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE STA 2025), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Lightweight Hybrid Model Based on Compact Convolutional Transformers (CCTs) for Efficient Skin Lesions Classification
journal: 2025 IEEE 22nd International Conference on Sciences and Techniques of Automatic Control and Computer Engineering (STA)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of deploying deep learning models for skin cancer diagnosis in resource-constrained clinical settings by proposing a lightweight hybrid architecture called the Compact Convolutional Transformer (CCT). The CCT model combines the local feature extraction of Convolutional Neural Networks with the global dependency modeling of Vision Transformers to efficiently classify dermoscopic images into benign and malignant categories. The authors evaluated the model on the HAM10000 dataset, utilizing data augmentation and a strict train-validation-test split to ensure robust generalization. Comparative analysis against benchmarks like ViT, EfficientNetV2, and standard CNNs demonstrated that the CCT achieved competitive diagnostic performance with an accuracy and F1-score of 0.85. Crucially, the CCT required only 4 million parameters, representing a parameter reduction of over 95% compared to the standard ViT, while incurring a marginal performance loss of only 1-2%. These results establish the CCT as a highly viable, efficient solution for automated skin lesion classification in low-resource environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant; the study focuses on binary classification of melanoma (malignant) vs. benign lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The test set contained 300 malignant cases.
Classified as benign; the study focuses on binary classification of benign vs. malignant lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The test set contained 360 benign cases.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images into benign and malignant categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin lesions classification' and 'binary classification task of skin cancer'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) using the HAM10000 dataset. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy (0.85), F1-score (0.85), precision, recall, and AUC (0.94). The output involves a softmax function to obtain probability distributions over the two classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A curated collection of dermoscopic images primarily sourced from the HAM10000 challenge dataset and a public repository on Kaggle (kmader/skin-cancer-mnist-ham10000). The images were standardized to a resolution of 224x224 pixels. The initial pool consisted of approximately 3,300 images after filtering/selection.

use.training: Used for training the Compact Convolutional Transformer (CCT) model after data augmentation (random rotation, horizontal/vertical flipping).
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor loss and accuracy.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation, reserved as 20% of the total dataset (660 images) and strictly kept unseen during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, F1-score, parameter count) without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The CCT achieved competitive performance with a marginal loss of 1-2% compared to ViT while reducing parameters by over 95%. The CCT had low inference latency and FLOPs. The study did not report statistical significance tests (p-values).

components: Convolutional Tokenizer (CCTTokenizer) with 7x7 kernel, stride 2, and ReLU activation
architecture_details.classifier_head: LayerNorm followed by a multilayer perceptron (MLP) and softmax function

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as a geometric transformation during the preprocessing stage to enhance model generalization and mitigate overfitting. Specific rotation angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied as a geometric transformation during the preprocessing stage. Specific probabilities were not reported.
Applied as a geometric transformation during the preprocessing stage. Specific probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed Compact Convolutional Transformer (CCT) model achieves a competitive diagnostic performance with an accuracy and F1-score of 0.85 on the HAM10000 dataset.
The CCT model requires only 4 million parameters, representing a parameter reduction of over 95% compared to the standard Vision Transformer (ViT) which uses 86 million parameters.
The CCT incurs a marginal performance loss of only 1-2% (0.01-0.02 in accuracy/F1-score) compared to the standard ViT (0.87 accuracy/F1-score).
The CCT exhibits superior deployment efficiency characterized by low FLOPs and low inference latency, making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.

The CCT effectively balances local feature extraction via CNNs with global dependency modeling via Transformers, enhancing data efficiency and generalization compared to large-scale architectures that tend to overfit on limited datasets.
The model demonstrated robust ability to distinguish between classes with a low number of false negatives (28) for the malignant class, which is critical for medical diagnosis.
The CCT achieved an AUC of 0.94, confirming excellent discriminatory ability across all classification thresholds.
The study concludes that the CCT is a highly viable and efficient solution for automated skin lesion classification in low-resource clinical settings where annotated datasets are constrained.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant) on the HAM10000 dataset; external validation on other datasets or multi-class classification (e.g., the full eight-class HAM10000) was not performed.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for the performance comparisons against benchmark models (ViT, EfficientNetV2, Standard CNN).
The dataset was curated from the HAM10000 challenge and a Kaggle repository, potentially introducing selection bias compared to the original diverse clinical sources.

The model was trained for only 10 epochs, which may be insufficient to fully converge compared to standard training protocols for Vision Transformers, though it highlights the model's data efficiency.
The paper acknowledges that while the CCT reduces parameters by over 95% compared to ViT, it incurs a marginal performance loss (1-2% in accuracy/F1-score).
The study focuses on resource-constrained environments; clinical relevance in high-resource settings where computational cost is less of a barrier is not addressed.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by a strict 80/20 train/validation/test split with augmentation applied only to the training set, but the small test set size (660 images) limits the robustness of the evaluation.
